channel.title: Musings
channel.description: Or, more likely, ramblings. By Michael Klepacki.

item.title: 04 April 2026 at 02:26 CEST
item.dc:creator: Michael Klepacki
item.description: Once more within the code well into the night, to add yet more, and through my headphones I can hear the rain pouring down, like a movie trope or like it did when last we spoke or like seemingly every afternoon that summer, rolling in over the water to batter the sand and lash against the hotel windows as it lashes now against mine. But there are no rainbows at this hour, no curves of brilliant colour to glimpse tonight; yet I will wait and watch because I hope, I pray, I vow, I swear: I will see another soon.

item.title: 02 April 2026 at 00:26 CEST
item.dc:creator: Michael Klepacki
item.description: The walls are sagging and warped and, possibly, rotting. The ceiling now sunk alarmingly low in the corner; oddly bowed upward near the door. The paint is bubbled and peeling, undoubtedly absorbing far too much moisture from the cold wet drafts that creep in around the rigid window frames. My foot keeps scuffing as I cross the room; the floor is too high here, no? And over there, why do the boards creak so? Always cold; again, the drafts, but — wait — is that a crack? Just here, arcing from floor to ceiling like a bolt of lightning, and where there is lightning there is thunder… yes; I hear it; rumbling, roaring, raging, I feel it. Can I patch this up? But the paint will peel anew and the cracks will grow and multiply until the walls are a hurricane because it is not the walls but lower, deeper. We built upon a bed of silt, or perhaps a bed of snakes. Indeed a miracle the walls have held this long. They may in fact hold for many years more, as the bubbles burst and blacken and the floorboards lift and splinter and the ceiling sags until you ponder if you should find something to prop it up as you work your way uphill to look out the window in wonder. As it all slowly crumbles, there, just outside: are the trees beginning to bloom?

item.title: 26 March 2026 at 23:08 CET
item.dc:creator: Michael Klepacki
item.description:
Am I an opportunist for leaving? Am I a coward for staying away? I’d dreamt of leaving for so long, hadn’t I? And now I wonder if I ever should’ve left; equally filled with a desperate gnawing homesickness and dogged conviction to never return. But why shouldn’t I? In fact — shouldn’t I? Shouldn’t I fight? Not by standing in a plaza half the world away with tens of other disgruntled deserters, but by — what, exactly? What of those still manning the ship; those I left behind? In these desperate, maybe dying, days, shouldn’t we pull close, closer than ever before? Shouldn’t we have done that all along?

It is unnatural, to be so far from everything you know. Oh, what of nature in this world; but still. I don’t belong here; I am unmoored; adrift. Perhaps I simply never learned to belong. Perhaps to search, to flee, is all I know.

item.title: 20 March 2026 at 19:49 CET
item.dc:creator: Michael Klepacki
item.description: “It ebbs and flows” sounds too nice. “It comes in waves” too peaceful. Today it is here. The unbearable weight. The numb detachment. The vague darkness. Today it is a parasite, leeching and sucking and draining. Teased by tears that do not come, dried and hollowed out. Tired of fatigue, lethargically blasé. Not a river, nor ocean, but perhaps a fog; imperceptibly rolling in, obscuring, chilling, isolating; then lifting as subtly as it came, driven by indiscernible changes in the dew point, whatever that is. How to raise the temperature, to alter the humidity? The things, the things to do, they do not always work; laughably imprecise tools. Yes, perhaps it is like the weather, elusive and ever-changing, driven by forces both too big and too small to suss out cause. I will not bother with a forecast. Tomorrow I will simply open the window.

item.title: 18 March 2026 at 23:17 CET
item.dc:creator: Michael Klepacki
item.description: The years are sneaky quiet. Without a sound the hair grows thinner, the skin dappled and soft. White strands multiply slowly and silently. The bags under the eyes a lost cause. The years are felt in the aches and the stiff and the creaks. They are heavy in the bones and heavy in the heart. They steal from the mind but they are clever tricky thieves. What charges to bear? Isn’t it all still there? Want is still want, pain still pain. Still awaiting the change that never seems to come; the switch to flip but there is no switch, is there? Only the years, the sneaky quiet years, slinking along, swift in the shadows.

item.title: 13 March 2026 at 00:37 CET
item.dc:creator: Michael Klepacki
item.description:
The internet is written in ink, or so they say, but memory is a finger brushed across a foggy pane of glass. It has a fickle half-life. Breathe on the glass again, play a song or catch a scent, and perhaps you can still discern the lines and shapes. For how long?

How can there still be so much ahead when I can already hardly remember so little? I feel as if I am wandering aimless in the fog, no sense of where I am going and barely a trace of from where I have come. Faces, moments, entire lives that I once held so dear, that once wrecked me, that once meant the world to me, conversations and laughter and discoveries and obsessions that made me, shaped me — nearly lost, now formless abstractions within the void.

As a child I would lie awake well into the night and try to picture death, to wrap my head around eternity. Eternal nonexistence — it was the scariest thing I could imagine; so unknowable and permanent; it kept me up for hours. But these days it’s trying to remember that keeps me up, that fills me with baffled dread — because most of me is already dead, gone into that dark abyss. How have I lost so much? As if I woke up one day to find my apartment half-empty, the theft only more violent by the fact that I never noticed, couldn’t tell you what was taken when. It isn’t fair; I want it all back. But the moisture is evaporating back into thin air, and time, hardly any, has wiped the pane clean and those crude strokes have disappeared, never to be seen again.

item.title: 10 March 2026 at 00:17 CET
item.description: The rain is about to come back and I don’t want it to though it is badly needed. The air has been hazy and brown-grey and dirty and sharp. The Seine is probably low and it’s been unseasonably, scarily warm and I love it even as it terrifies me. I am a reptile happy to sit upon a warm rock in the blinding noon until I am dizzy and drunk from the sun. The rain does not suit me, though once I longed for it, found an animalistic thrill in the wild august storms, the thunderous blue-hour downpours, the sky ripped open, fury and passion unleashed. But here this is no fury and there is no passion, almost never; no, there is just a steady monotonous unrelenting drizzle — if that — sometimes just a pervasive drifting mist. And once I dreamt of that, too, dreamt of any break from the dirt and the bright and the blue, dreamt of foggy green hills and cloud cover and cool air. But the green bears a cost. You must pay in warmth and light and joy, you must pay, pay dearly, pay until you are left soggy and sodden and downtrodden, day after day after day until you fear the light will blind you upon its return but you will gladly take the blindness for the warmth on your face. And anyway the green is not even here because here it is all stone and brick, all unnatural beige and white and grey, towers and walls of it, an endless labyrinth, and I am lost.

item.title: 07 March 2026 at 02:36 CET
item.description:
Lately I’ve been feeling restless. Wanting to stay up all night. It’s the depression, probably. I used to stay up for hours and hours into the night as a teenager. Usually on tumblr, or working on homework I had put off. These days I’m probably fiddling with this site (at best) or doomscrolling (not best, obviously).

It’s the depression, probably. But the reason I’m turning here, to this site surely no one but me sees, maybe, is because I’m watching a space I grew up loving be sucked of it’s soul. I used to love the web. It was somehow both a safe haven and an actual wild west within my sheltered desert home. It was where I raised Neopets until my mother needed the phone. Where I chatted on AIM with my improbable long-distance girlfriend. Where I discovered Homestar Runner and its ilk, leading to in-jokes with friends and my first ill-advised email address. Where I played games that required flash like slime basketball as my best friend sat next to me and we shared the keyboard. Where I sailed the Limewire seas to build up my music collection, because music was my best escape from the darkness but I was an adolescent with no allowance.1

But they are ruining the web. You know of whom I speak. They are stripping it of all humanity, all craft, all heart. Every day it is harder to find anything not devoid of humanity, anything you can trust, any place where you are not simply a collection of data points to be monetised. Content is king but content is dead; long live slop. They are destroying the place I love and I am angry.

And yet. I am heartened by those who still believe; who still handcraft and defend their own spaces and homes online; those who astound and inspire me with their knowledge of modern CSS and Javascript; those whose sites remind me of the AIM days; who write about music, the last films they watched, what’s inspiring them, what’s making them sad; who draw and code and read and share and type and strive and tinker and fiddle; those who fight; those who blog.2 I know I am not alone in my anger and my heart swells, if briefly, and I too want to fight.

And so… a blog. If you are here, thank you. If you’ve made it this far, hello, friend. I hope I can keep this up. Let’s fight together.

  1. Attn FBI: that’s a fictional artistic flourish, of course. ↑

  2. People like ahn and Silvia and Gabo and Robin Spielmann and Robin Rendle and Louie Mantia and Mandy Brown and Henry from Online. ↑
